---
title: "About Me"
about:
  template: trestles 
  image: "images/Christopher 05.jpg"
  image-shape: round
  links:
    - icon: envelope
      text: Email
      href: contact.qmd
    - text: "<img src='images/linkedin.png' width='70' height='16'>"
      href: https://linkedin.com/in/christopher-maymon 
    - text: "<img src='images/github.png' width='60' height='16'>"
      href: https://github.com/themaymon
    - text: "<img src='images/scholar.png' width='20' height='16'>"
      href: https://scholar.google.com/citations?user=RjwGrqMAAAAJ&hl=en&oi=ao
    - text: "<img src='images/osf.png' width='40' height='16'>"
      href: https://osf.io/6tvfs/    
    - text: "<img src='images/orcid.png' width='40' height='16'>"
      href: https://orcid.org/0000-0003-3837-7674
    - text: "<img src='images/bluesky.png' width='70' height='16'>"
      href: https://bsky.app/profile/christophermaymon.bsky.social
    - text: "<img src='images/researchgate.png' width='90' height='16'>"
      href: https://www.researchgate.net/profile/Christopher-Maymon-3?ev=hdr_xprf
---

## Science is always a story about people

My life began in 1991, in the city of Warwick, Rhode Island. I am the first-born child to working class parents. When I was two, I would be joined by my sister, and five years later, my sister and I would be persuaded to accept the arrival of our brother.

My earliest memories place me aboard my maternal grandfather's sailboat. As a toddler, I learned how to tie bow-lines, bait lures for bass, and read the channel markers throughout Greenwich Bay. But amongst these practical lessons, what my grandfather taught me was how to deduce. He was a man who had fastened together a life from the products of his own hands and grit. He was a public school science teacher, and he ensured that my earliest notions about the world were always accompanied by empirical questions. 

For six years, we moved away from the familiarity of Rhode Island's coastal inlets, north to the ancient forests of New Hampshire. In these years, I was a free-range child, spending my days wandering with my dog through forgotten logging trails, crossing nameless rivers, buildiing caches and shelters, some of which I suspect survives to this very day. But it was in those woods that I first began to appreciate the company of my imagination. This layer of bespoke reality I could drape across my surroundings became not only a platform for exploration, but thanks to the questions my grandfather had given me, also a subject of scientific curiosity. And so began my journey exploring the mysteries of the mind. 

## The unfolding shape of one's career

The story of my professional training a decade, working in several disciplines across psychology. 

In 2013, I graduated from Loyola University of Maryland with a BA in Psychology. I was then employed at the Pediatric Feeding Disorders Unit at The Kennedy Krieger Institute, where I was trained to implement, teach, and refine treatment protocols using principles of Applied Behaviour Analysis. 

In 2015, I moved to Aotearoa to pursue a PhD in Cognitive Developmental Psychology at Te Herenga Waka | Victoria University of Wellington. In my doctoral research, I developed an ecologically valid behavioural test capable of disentangling competing theoretical accounts about the cognitive mechanisms that allow humans to track the content of other people's minds. 

### The Experimental Psychology Trade-Off

Following graduation, my research interests evolved again. I started to focus on how experimental psychology has confronted *ecological validity* (i.e., the extent to which laboratory findings can inform us about phenomena that occurs outside the laboratory). I became increasingly skeptical of what was historically considered an inescapable trade-off: that it is not possible to have complete control over experimental conditions without stripping those conditiosn of their verisimilitude (i.e., the extent to which laboratory conditions replicate conditions of the real-world). My central thesis was that that this apparent trade-off was not a *logical* incompatibility, but simply a *practical* challenge, wanting for a technological solution. I further believed that the technological solution had just come to market, in the form of the virtual reality headset. From 2020 to 2023, I speerheaded the development of a virtual reality laboratory for the advancement of a more *ecologically valid experimental psychology*. 

In 2020, I 

### Open Science & Reproducibility

As I built more complex simulations, I realized another problem: **Reproducibility.**

A text-based methods section in a PDF cannot possibly capture the complexity of a VR environment. If I say "the participant stood on a ledge," how high was it? How was the lighting? What was the physics engine doing?

This realization turned me into a fierce advocate for **Open Science**. I believe that code *is* the method. Today, I publish my datasets, analysis code (R/Python), and VR environments (Unity) openly. I advocate for a science where "replicating a study" means downloading the environment and hitting 'Play'.

### Current Work

Today, I wear three hats:
1.  **Lecturer:** Teaching the next generation of psychologists how to use these tools. I teach courses on Experimental Design, VR Development, and Data Analysis.
2.  **Researcher:** Investigating research questions about emotions, emotion regulations, the relationship between emotion and cognition, and how our minds construct our reality.
3.  **Consultant:** Helping other labs and companies build valid, reliable, and rigorous VR experiments.

If you are interested in discussing experimental design, VR development, or data analysis, please [get in touch](contact.qmd).
